top_entities.name: Ukraine
top_entities.news_summary: CEASEFIRE DAY 3 FINAL CLOSE — highest-signal scoring event in the window. Russian drones, bombs and artillery struck civilian areas of Kharkiv and Kherson on May 11, killing at least 2 people and wounding 7 including a 14-year-old boy (PBS). Both sides traded blame as the 72-hour ceasefire neared expiry. Ukraine's conduct: no confirmed Ukrainian-attributed breach across all three ceasefire days — Ukraine maintained no-breach posture throughout. PRISONER EXCHANGE: Talks continued through May 11 but the full 1,000-for-1,000 exchange was NOT completed by ceasefire expiry. Zelenskyy submitted Ukraine's 1,000-prisoner list but exchange remained in coordination phase. US was to 'guarantee' the swap; completion unconfirmed at day-3 close. Putin accused Ukraine of not being ready (disputed by Kyiv). Ceasefire expired with fighting persisting and exchange incomplete. ISW confirmed Russia used ceasefire to pull forward reserves and increase drone strikes (stockpile window exploitation). Victory Day ceasefire expired May 11.
top_entities.assessor_note: FINAL CEASEFIRE WINDOW ASSESSMENT REQUIRED. Three-day conduct record is now complete. Ukraine's side: no confirmed breach across days 1, 2, or 3. Ukraine submitted 1,000-prisoner list (ACT signal). Prisoner exchange incomplete at ceasefire expiry — classify as 'prepared and submitted but not executed'; this is a partial ACT credit — Ukraine fulfilled its list-submission obligation; failure to execute attributable to coordination breakdown and Russia's disputed readiness. Day-3 breach on Ukrainian side: none confirmed by independent sources. ISW finding that Russia used ceasefire for stockpiling is an INT-relevant observation — it deepens the bad-faith classification of Russia's conduct without creating a new Ukrainian breach. Score Ukraine: INT (conditional credit for 3-day breach-free conduct), ACT (partial credit for list submission; execution incomplete). The 50.0 hold score should now be re-evaluated against the full 3-day evidence set. Final score determination is the assessor's responsibility.

top_entities.name: Russia
top_entities.news_summary: Day-3 ceasefire breach confirmed: Russian drones, bombs and artillery struck Kharkiv and Kherson civilian areas on May 11, killing at least 2, wounding 7 including a 14-year-old boy. 38 Russian attacks on Ukrainian positions reported by 16:00 (General Staff). ISW assessment: Russia used the Victory Day ceasefire as a stockpile window — drone strikes rose during truce (pre-positioned Molniya drones deployed); reserves moved forward in Lyman direction. Russia's MoD continued 'strict observance' claims while strikes proceeded — bad-faith-INT pattern documented across all 3 ceasefire days. Putin claimed Ukraine not ready for prisoner exchange; Kyiv denied this. Exchange not completed by ceasefire expiry. Cumulative 6-event ceasefire breach record: 3 unilateral collapses (May 5-7), 3 trilateral breaches (days 1-2-3: Kharkiv strike, 3 killed/19 injured, 2 killed/7 injured). ISW stockpile-window exploitation adds a strategic bad-faith dimension beyond individual strikes.
top_entities.assessor_note: Floor entity — conduct documentation only. No score change. The 3-day ceasefire conduct record is now complete: Russia breached across all 3 trilateral ceasefire days (plus 3 prior unilateral collapses = 6 total). ISW stockpile-window finding is the most analytically significant day-3 addition — Russia not only continued strikes but strategically exploited the ceasefire format to reposition materiel. This confirms the ceasefire was treated as a tactical opportunity, not a good-faith humanitarian pause. Document: (1) Day-3 civilian strikes (2 killed, 7 injured, Kharkiv/Kherson); (2) ISW stockpile-window exploitation; (3) Prisoner exchange blame-shifting continued on day 3 (Putin accused Ukraine of unreadiness). Cumulative 6-event breach record is now the standing INT floor-conduct input for Russia's ceasefire window.

top_entities.name: Hungary
top_entities.news_summary: THREE-DAY INAUGURATION WINDOW CLOSES. Day-3 summary: Hungarian President Tamas Sulyok signed decrees appointing 16 ministers on May 12 (cabinet formation effectively complete at end of 3-day window). Ministers sworn in May 13. Cabinet includes: Foreign Minister Anita Orbán (diplomat), Economy/Energy Minister Kapitány (former Shell), Finance Minister Kármán (former Erste Bank). 16 ministries — up from 12 under Orbán — with separate health, environmental protection, and education ministries. Magyar's Sulyok resignation demand (May 31 deadline) confirmed active. Fidesz condemned demand as 'unworthy of a prime minister.' Sulyok has NOT resigned as of day-3 close. EU fund plan submission date May 27 confirmed. Magyar government now fully constituted at 3-day window close — all key structural reform commitments from day-1 assessment remain active and unreverted. No negative regression from days 1-2-3 evidence.
top_entities.assessor_note: Day-3 inauguration window is now closed. Evidence summary: Cabinet fully constituted (Sulyok signed decrees May 12; ministers sworn in May 13 — just outside the May 11 window but within the 3-day process). 16 ministries including new standalone health, environment, and education portfolios — structural reform signal. Sulyok resignation demand (May 31) active; Sulyok has not resigned; Fidesz opposes. EU funds plan submission May 27 confirmed. The May 9 proposal (28.1 → 37.5, +9.4, Developing retained) should be confirmed — day-3 evidence provides no new scoring inputs above day-1 baseline but confirms no reversal. Cabinet formation completion is the enacted structural signal that supports the +9.4 award. June 9 is the 30-day reassessment. Correction note: band path is Developing → Developing (both 28.1 and 37.5 are in Developing band 21-40). Do NOT label as Critical → Developing.

top_entities.name: Israel
top_entities.news_summary: Gaza conflict ongoing. May 11 reporting: civilian casualties continue; airstrikes, shelling, and gunfire across all Gaza areas per UNRWA. OHCHR torture/inhumane-treatment allegations from flotilla detention (35 individuals, May 8 OHCHR press release) remain unaddressed — deportation of last two detained activists (Abukeshek, Avila, deported May 10) resolved the detention-duration event but not the conduct-during-detention accountability. UNRWA reports since March 2025 Israel blocked UNRWA from directly bringing humanitarian personnel and aid into Gaza. Monthly casualty data continuing. Reason.com genocide framing debate piece published May 11 — not primary-source evidence; note only. Total confirmed casualties as of May 11: 72,740+ (20,179 children) per Palestinian Ministry of Health.
top_entities.assessor_note: Floor entity — conduct documentation update only. No score change. OHCHR torture/inhumane-treatment allegations (35 flotilla detainees, May 8) remain the priority open conduct category — deportation of last activists (May 10) does not address allegations. UNRWA access block (since March 2025) is a continuing humanitarian-access conduct category. Casualty accumulation (72,740+) is an ongoing count. Floor conduct record: add day-3 documentation note. No structural change events that would trigger floor-exit criteria review.

top_entities.name: Pakistan
top_entities.news_summary: 1-year India-Pakistan conflict anniversary (May 7-11): Pakistan military warned of strong response to any Indian attack; security tightened in Islamabad. India's armed forces marked Operation Sindoor anniversary with warnings to Pakistan. Al Jazeera analysis: ceasefire holding at 1 year, but punitive non-military measures remain (suspended visas, closed airspace, trade ban, Attari-Wagah border closed, Indus Waters Treaty suspended by India). No new escalation confirmed in May 10-11 window. HRCP 2025 report: marked deterioration in judicial independence (27th Constitutional Amendment, executive control over judicial appointments); media freedom crackdown; 531,700+ Afghan deportations. Boundary watch: composite 20.3, 0.3 above Critical floor (20.0).
top_entities.assessor_note: HOLD — assessed May 8. Anniversary tensions (mutual military warnings) are in-window but do not constitute a new structural scoring event — the ceasefire has held for 1 year and no new kinetic incident occurred. HRCP judicial independence deterioration is a structural signal but predates the May 10-11 window (carry-over from early May report). Do not reassess. Maintain boundary watch at 20.3. Input the HRCP judicial-independence finding (27th Constitutional Amendment) as a carry-over for the next Pakistan rotation assessment.

IOM (May 2026): 3 years of war, famine continues, aid system under pressure. Sudan continues as floor entity with no structural change signal.
top_entities.assessor_note: Floor entity structural-change scan only. Famine confirmed (multiple areas), aid visa obstruction confirmed (110/355 approvals), CRSV documented (330+ cases). top_entities.name: South Sudan
top_entities.news_summary: HRW World Report 2026: 10 million people in South Sudan need humanitarian assistance; 7.6 million projected at crisis food insecurity. UN expressed concern over government order directing civilians and aid agencies to vacate Akobo (270,000 displaced people host town) ahead of military operations. May 3: government forces bombarded hospital in Fangak (Jonglei state) — 7 killed, 20+ injured. ICRC: humanitarian needs continue to rise. No structural governance change.
top_entities.assessor_note: Floor entity structural-change scan only. Hospital bombardment (Fangak, May 3) is a new conduct event since last assessment (May 5) — document in floor conduct record. Trump said deal is 'possible' (CNBC April 21). Pentagon still lists Anthropic as 'supply chain risk' as of May 1. Pentagon May 1 announced deals with Nvidia, Microsoft, AWS, Reflection AI, SpaceX, OpenAI, Google (7 companies) — Anthropic excluded. White House re-engagement is a potential carve-out/governance signal: Anthropic holding its safety red lines (no autonomous weapons, no mass surveillance) while White House considers exceptions. This complex picture should be scored in full on May 15.
top_entities.assessor_note: HOLD — do not assess until May 15. Pre-stage evidence collected: White House reopened talks (April 29 Axios); Trump said deal 'possible' (April 21); Pentagon blacklist maintained through May 1; Anthropic excluded from May 1 Pentagon AI deals; Mythos tool carve-out noted (not a safeguard-compliance event). The scoring picture for May 15: Anthropic is holding its safety red lines (no autonomous weapons, no mass surveillance) while the administration explores exceptions. The federal court blocking of the contract cancellation is a legal dimension. Safety-red-line adherence is a positive ACC/INT signal. Pentagon exclusion and 'supply chain risk' label are structural ACC negative signals. Full assessment required May 15.

top_entities.name: Waymo
top_entities.news_summary: NEAR-WINDOW RECALL — NOTE ONLY. Waymo filed voluntary recall of 3,791 robotaxis on May 12 (just outside May 10-11 window) to fix software issue: a vehicle drove on a flooded road in San Antonio (incident April 20). Waymo paused operations in affected city, filed voluntary recall within 10 days, deploying OTA fix to entire fleet. Separate Koreatown (LA) rollover crash May 11 (in-window): Waymo robotaxi involved in rollover with white sedan at 3:10 a.m.; no injuries reported. Prior context: Waymo band crossing proposal (Functional → Established) pending human review from May 6. This recall and crash are safety conduct signals relevant to the pending assessment.
top_entities.assessor_note: Flag for assessor attention: Waymo has a pending band-crossing proposal from May 6 (Functional → Established). New safety data: (1) Koreatown rollover crash May 11 (in-window) — no injuries, but incident confirmed. (2) Voluntary recall May 12 (3,791 vehicles, flooded-road software issue) — voluntary filing and OTA fix are positive transparency/governance signals. The voluntary recall is a positive safety-governance signal (proactive disclosure, rapid response). The flooding incident (April 20) preceded recall filing by 22 days — response time acceptable per NHTSA standards. Assessor must weigh: does voluntary recall + OTA fix strengthen or weaken the Functional → Established case? Recommendation: the rapid voluntary response is consistent with the Established band's higher governance standards, not inconsistent.
